mappings.rationaleAI: Directly addresses clouds, precipitation, storms, El Nino, numerical weather prediction (C.2), weather forecasting (C), and chaos theory's role in predictability limits (C.4.b).
Explores climate types, temperature patterns, atmospheric pressure, rainfall distribution (A.4.c), and the factors that generate climate (E.1).
Directly addresses climatic change (E.6), greenhouse warming from CO2 buildup (E.2.c), extreme weather events, and evidence for historical climate shifts.
Examines atmospheric moisture, the greenhouse effect (E.1.a.ii), and the electromagnetic spectrum relevant to solar radiation absorption that drives climate (E.1.a).
Addresses climate sensitivity, the greenhouse effect (E.1.a.ii), solar energy input (E.1.a), and carbon dioxide's role in climate change (E.2.c).
Directly addresses hurricanes/typhoons (B.4), storms (B.1-B.3), floods from extreme precipitation (A.4.d), and wildfire linked to climate.
Solar radiation variability and the solar constant (E.1.a.i) are directly relevant to solar radiation as a primary factor generating climate.
Chaos theory and sensitivity to initial conditions are directly relevant to limits of weather predictability (C.4.b) and numerical weather modeling (C.2).
Glacial-interglacial climate cycles and ice core evidence are directly relevant to climatic change evidence from glaciations and lake/sea level fluctuations (E.6.a).
Arid climates, rain shadow effects, and evaporation are relevant to climatic types and their distribution (E.3) and local terrain effects (E.2.b).
Human-driven climate change from fossil fuel burning and land use change is directly relevant to human effects on global climate (E.2.c).
Mountain weather systems, orographic precipitation effects, and local terrain modification of climate (E.2.b) including altitude-driven climatic variation.
The relationship between climate zones and the distribution of life forms is relevant to influences of climate on terrestrial life (E.4) and climatic types (E.3).
